---
title: "Inclusion of diversity in AD research"
subtitle: "GBM6330E – Final Project"
root: "."
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    include-after-body:
      - text: "<script src=\"{{< meta root >}}/assets/js/scroll-spy.js\"></script>"
---

<!-- Sidebar Navigation -->
```{=html}
<nav class="sidebar-nav">
  <a href="#home" title="Home">Home</a>
  <a href="#part1" title="Part I">Part I</a>
  <a href="#part2" title="Part II">Part II</a>
  <a href="#research" title="Research">Research</a>
</nav>
<div class="content-main">
```

<!-- HOME SECTION -->
<section class="scroll-section" id="home">

::: {.hero}
# {{< var project_title >}}

<div class="badge-soft">GBM6330E • Final Project</div>

<p class="lead">
This site is a structured, reproducible project on why and how <strong>diversity</strong> must be included in research that aims to prevent and diagnose Alzheimer's disease (AD), and how current initiatives and datasets address (or fail to address) this need.
</p>

**How to use this site**
- **Part I** is a guided narrative (definitions, heterogeneity, diversity dimensions, and scope).
- **Part II** is an exploration interface with an **Interactive Explorer** (map + modality diagram), and supporting pages on datasets, atlases/harmonisation, open science, and reproducibility.
- **Original research** links to three reproducible repositories (to be created) corresponding to your project's research deliverables.

:::

::: {.card-grid}

::: {.card}
### Part I — The need for diversity
Why inclusion matters: what AD is, why it is heterogeneous, and which diversity dimensions impact prevention and diagnosis.

[Explore Part I](#part1){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.card}
### Part II — Interactive Explorer
Explore datasets and initiatives by **geography** (continent/country) and **data modality** (neuroimaging, genetics/proteomics, exposomics).

[Explore Part II](#part2){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.card}
### Original research
Three reproducible sub-projects (each intended as its own GitHub repo), linked from the hub site.

[View Research](#research){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

:::

</section>

<!-- PART I SECTION -->
<section class="scroll-section" id="part1">

## Part I – The Need for Diversity

This section guides you through the fundamental concepts underlying this project.

### Defining Alzheimer's disease and the specificity of AD

::: {.callout-note}
This page is a **scaffold**. Replace placeholder text with your own writing and add citations (e.g., to clinical definitions, diagnostic criteria, and epidemiology sources).
:::

#### What is Alzheimer's disease?

*Placeholder:* Define AD as a neurodegenerative disease and clarify how it relates to the clinical syndrome of dementia.

Consider covering:
- Clinical presentation and typical trajectory
- Neuropathology (e.g., amyloid and tau) vs. clinical diagnosis
- Biomarker frameworks and how they impact "early" diagnosis

#### Why definitions matter for diversity

*Placeholder:* Explain how case definitions, recruitment criteria, and diagnostic thresholds can create systematic inclusion/exclusion biases.

Examples to discuss:
- Differential access to biomarker testing
- Cultural/linguistic effects on cognitive testing
- Comorbidities that vary by population and affect diagnosis

---

### A highly heterogeneous disease

*Placeholder text on disease heterogeneity and why population diversity is critical for understanding different presentations.*

---

### Defining diversity that impacts AD

*Placeholder text defining different dimensions of diversity (ancestry, socioeconomic, geographic, sex/gender, age, comorbidities) and their relevance to AD research.*

---

### State of the art, scope & motivation

*Placeholder text on current state of AD research and gaps this project aims to address.*

</section>

<!-- PART II SECTION -->
<section class="scroll-section" id="part2">

## Part II – Initiatives Integrating Diversity

Explore datasets, atlases, and open-science initiatives addressing diversity in AD research.

### Global Overview

![World map showing continental distribution of AD research initiatives and datasets]({{< meta root >}}/assets/img/world-map.svg){width=100% fig-alt="World map with continents highlighted showing the scope of this review"}

This map visualizes the geographic scope of this project, highlighting the continents and regions where AD research with diversity considerations is emerging or established.

**Why Geographic Distribution Matters**

- **Data availability** varies dramatically by region
- **Research equity** requires intentional inclusion from underrepresented populations
- **Biological diversity** (ancestry, environmental exposures) enriches our understanding of AD heterogeneity
- **Resource constraints** differ globally, affecting infrastructure for data harmonisation

---

### Interactive Explorer

Use the **Interactive Explorer** to explore specific datasets, research initiatives, and geographic regions:

- **Datasets** by country and modality
- **Geographic focus** by continent
- **Research initiatives** and harmonisation pipelines
- **Modality-specific data** (neuroimaging, genetics, proteomics, exposomics)

[Open Interactive Explorer](part2/explorer.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}

---

### Datasets Overview

A comprehensive catalog of AD datasets with information on modalities, geographic coverage, access requirements, and diversity metrics.

[View Datasets Catalog](part2/datasets.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}

---

### Shared Atlases & Harmonisation

Documentation of shared neuroimaging and multimodal atlases, harmonisation pipelines, and standardization efforts in AD research.

[View Harmonisation Initiatives](part2/atlases-harmonization.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}

---

### Open Science & Collaboration

Current initiatives, consortia, and collaborative frameworks promoting open science practices in AD research.

[Learn About Initiatives](part2/initiatives.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}

---

### Reproducibility in AD Research

Analysis of code and data sharing practices in AD research publications.

[View Reproducibility Study](part2/reproducibility.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}

</section>

<!-- ORIGINAL RESEARCH SECTION -->
<section class="scroll-section" id="research">

## Original Research – Reproducible Sub-Projects

This hub site is designed so each original research deliverable lives in its **own GitHub repository** (reproducible, with its own README, data dictionary, and—if applicable—code + environment specification).

Update the links below once you create those repositories.

::: {.card-grid}

::: {.card}
### 1) Open documentation: AD datasets catalog
Create an open documentation to recap all available AD datasets and their content (as exhaustive as possible).

- **Format:** GitHub repository + issues for community contributions
- **Recommended structure:** data dictionary, modality tags, access requirements, geographies, demographics reporting checklist

[Repo link (update)](https://github.com/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.card}
### 2) Open documentation: shared atlases & harmonisation pipelines
Create an open documentation listing shared atlases and harmonisation pipelines (neuroimaging + multimodal).

- **Format:** GitHub repository + versioned inventory
- **Recommended fields:** modality, license, citation, scope, input/output, validation benchmarks

[Repo link (update)](https://github.com/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.card}
### 3) Reproducibility study: code/data sharing in *Alzheimer's & Dementia*
Investigate the proportion of papers sharing code and datasets in the journal.

- **Format:** reproducible repo (data extraction protocol + analysis scripts + outputs)
- **Include:** PRISMA-like flow, inter-rater reliability, open dataset of metadata where permitted

[Repo link (update)](https://github.com/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

:::

---

## What to edit first

1. Update `data/datasets.json` with your real catalog (as exhaustive as possible).
2. Update `data/region_summaries.json` with your writing + citations.
3. Replace placeholder text in Part I and Part II narrative pages with your analysis.

> Tip: The Interactive Explorer will automatically reflect updates to these JSON files.

---

## Data sources & attribution

[View Attribution & Sources](attribution.qmd){.btn .btn-secondary}

</section>

```{=html}
</div>
```
